criterion_breakdown.label: Durable truth and legal-state integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Strongest alignment with the explicit control-store and append-only-evidence split in the corpus, while keeping mutable legal truth out of transport and cache layers.

criterion_breakdown.label: Replayability and deterministic reconstruction
criterion_breakdown.note: Supports exact replay and proof reconstruction well because manifests, freezes, hash sets, and evidence refs stay durable and queryable under one transactional spine.

criterion_breakdown.label: Append-only evidence and lineage integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Fits append-only audit and provenance semantics naturally without promoting the audit ledger into the only mutable system-of-record plane.

criterion_breakdown.label: Retention, erasure, and privacy reconciliation fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Separates mutable retention control from immutable evidence and blob bodies, which makes limitation notes, tombstones, and compensating re-erasure easier to preserve lawfully.

criterion_breakdown.label: Read-model rebuildability and stream/reconnect support
criterion_breakdown.note: Explicitly treats read models, queues, and caches as disposable while still supporting reconnect-safe SSE and route-scoped recovery.

criterion_breakdown.label: Authority idempotency, ingress, and resend safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Best fit for durable request envelopes, ingress receipts, interaction control records, and resend legality because those facts live outside the transport layer.

criterion_breakdown.label: Large artifact, quarantine, and export handling
criterion_breakdown.note: Object storage for immutable bodies and relational metadata for staging, request binding, and publish-time state cleanly match the upload and proof-bundle contracts.

criterion_breakdown.label: Migration, rollback, and fail-forward compatibility
criterion_breakdown.note: The expand-migrate-contract posture maps well onto relational truth plus rebuildable projections and outbox-mediated fanout.

criterion_breakdown.label: Restore speed, backup posture, and operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Tiered backup posture is straightforward: directly back up control, audit, and authoritative blob stores; rebuild queues, projections, and caches from them.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cost and operational simplicity
criterion_breakdown.note: Not the cheapest single-service design, but it avoids more dangerous complexity from conflating truth, evidence, and delivery.

criterion_breakdown.label: Scale and latency headroom under mixed workloads
criterion_breakdown.note: Good fit for the expected mixed workload, with room to scale projection and delivery paths independently without moving truth into the broker.

alternatives.label: Relational-first control store with append-only audit ledger, object store, broker, and cache/resume store
risks: Needs discipline around transactional outbox/inbox, append-only audit boundaries, and projection rebuild tooling.
Requires the team to operate multiple logical store roles instead of collapsing everything into one database or one stream.
strengths: Best fit for the corpus distinction between durable legal truth, append-only evidence, disposable projections, and transport-only delivery.
Supports no-blind-resend authority recovery because request identity, ingress receipts, and interaction control stay durable outside the broker.
Lets projections, caches, and SSE fanout rebuild from receipts, manifests, audit events, and object refs instead of becoming accidental truth.
alternatives.summary: Keep mutable legal truth in a transactional relational control store, append-only evidence in a distinct audit ledger, immutable bodies in object storage, transport in a queue/broker, and resumability in disposable cache stores. Use transactional outbox/inbox ledgers so broker loss is recoverable from durable truth.

criterion_breakdown.label: Durable truth and legal-state integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Can hold operational truth, but it is a weaker fit for the corpus's structured legal-state, receipt, and migration-control relationships than a transactional relational core.

criterion_breakdown.label: Replayability and deterministic reconstruction
criterion_breakdown.note: Replay is workable, but hash-bound basis and cross-artifact historical readability require more bespoke discipline than the relational-first option.

criterion_breakdown.label: Append-only evidence and lineage integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Append-only evidence can be modeled, but keeping immutable audit lineage distinct from mutable document updates is less natural.

criterion_breakdown.label: Retention, erasure, and privacy reconciliation fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Blob/body separation still works, but lifecycle and tombstone semantics become more subtle when mutable documents and evidence history coexist in one operational plane.

criterion_breakdown.label: Read-model rebuildability and stream/reconnect support
criterion_breakdown.note: Change feeds help projection fanout, though the system still needs explicit rebuild rules for compacted or superseded history.

criterion_breakdown.label: Authority idempotency, ingress, and resend safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Durable request identity and ingress proof can be preserved, but the authority lineage model fits less cleanly than in a relational control store with explicit append-only tables.

criterion_breakdown.label: Large artifact, quarantine, and export handling
criterion_breakdown.note: Blob attachments plus document metadata work well for uploads, exports, and proof bodies.

criterion_breakdown.label: Migration, rollback, and fail-forward compatibility
criterion_breakdown.note: Schema change is often easier operationally, but the corpus explicitly needs stronger reader-window and historical-bundle guarantees than schemaless drift tolerates comfortably.

criterion_breakdown.label: Restore speed, backup posture, and operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Restore can be decent, but proving audit continuity, replay-safe historical reads, and migration chronology is less direct.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cost and operational simplicity
criterion_breakdown.note: Simpler than the event-log-first option, but still not as normatively aligned as the relational-first control plane.

criterion_breakdown.label: Scale and latency headroom under mixed workloads
criterion_breakdown.note: Good read-side scaling characteristics, especially for denormalized route-facing state.

alternatives.label: Document-store-centric operational truth with change-feed projections and blob attachments
risks: Weaker fit for relational integrity around submission state, idempotency buckets, migration ledgers, and audit-link-heavy truth classes.
Makes replay, historical reader windows, and append-only evidence boundaries harder to explain cleanly than a relational-first control spine.
strengths: Keeps the number of durable state systems lower than a full event-log-first design.
Handles hierarchical read models and blob references comfortably for product-facing surfaces.
Can scale fanout and change-feed-driven projections reasonably well.
alternatives.summary: Store most mutable operational state in a document database, keep large bodies in object storage, project read models from change feeds, and use queue/broker infrastructure for asynchronous work and notifications.

criterion_breakdown.label: Durable truth and legal-state integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Conflicts with the explicit rule that broker/log transport must not become legal truth for manifests, workflow state, or authority settlement.

criterion_breakdown.label: Replayability and deterministic reconstruction
criterion_breakdown.note: Event replay is naturally strong, but the corpus needs more than event reprocessing: it needs stable basis hashes, request envelopes, blob refs, and historical reader-window guarantees.

criterion_breakdown.label: Append-only evidence and lineage integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Very strong on append-only history, which is the main advantage of this topology.

criterion_breakdown.label: Retention, erasure, and privacy reconciliation fit
criterion_breakdown.note: Erasure, tombstones, and proof-preserving limitation posture are possible, but retention-limited lifecycle rules become materially harder once the stream is treated as primary truth.

criterion_breakdown.label: Read-model rebuildability and stream/reconnect support
criterion_breakdown.note: Strongest fanout and projection story on paper, but it solves the easiest part of the problem better than the hardest part.

criterion_breakdown.label: Authority idempotency, ingress, and resend safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Authority sends and ingress checkpoints still need durable control records outside the broker, otherwise recovery drifts toward blind replay and ambiguous reconciliation.

criterion_breakdown.label: Large artifact, quarantine, and export handling
criterion_breakdown.note: Blob bodies still land in object storage, though their operational metadata tends to collapse back into a non-stream control store anyway.

criterion_breakdown.label: Migration, rollback, and fail-forward compatibility
criterion_breakdown.note: Schema evolution on a canonical event log is possible, but the reader-window and restore guarantees become much harder to keep simple and provable.

criterion_breakdown.label: Restore speed, backup posture, and operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Can recover projections quickly, but durable reopen safety still requires reconstructed control truth and explicit privacy plus authority gates.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cost and operational simplicity
criterion_breakdown.note: The operational burden is high once event compatibility, log retention, materialized views, blob refs, and legal correction flows all need first-class governance.

criterion_breakdown.label: Scale and latency headroom under mixed workloads
criterion_breakdown.note: Best theoretical headroom for high-throughput fanout, but that is not the dominant phase-00 constraint.

alternatives.label: Event-stream-first log promoted toward primary truth with downstream materialized views
risks: Directly fights the corpus requirement that the queue/broker not become the system of record for legal truth.
Harder to satisfy no-blind-resend authority recovery, explicit ingestion checkpoints, and restore reopen gating without introducing a second durable control plane anyway.
strengths: Excellent append history and fanout characteristics.
Strong fit for high-volume projection pipelines and reprocessing mechanics when the stream is the central axis.
Can make derived analytical timelines and materialized views feel very natural.
alternatives.summary: Promote the event stream or broker log toward primary persistence, model transactional state primarily through immutable events, and derive read models plus legal-state projections downstream from the stream.

criteria.label: Durable truth and legal-state integrity
criteria.rationale: Legal workflow state, submission state, manifests, receipts, and migration control objects need one authoritative durable home instead of being inferred from transport or projections.

criteria.label: Replayability and deterministic reconstruction
criteria.rationale: Exact replay, proof reconstruction, and restore verification depend on durable execution basis, graph lineage, and immutable artifact references that can be reloaded without transport guesswork.

criteria.label: Append-only evidence and lineage integrity
criteria.rationale: Audit evidence, provenance edges, authority envelopes, and replay attestations must remain historically traversable even when read models rebuild or releases roll back.

criteria.label: Retention, erasure, and privacy reconciliation fit
criteria.rationale: The topology has to preserve basis hashes, typed limitation posture, erasure proof, and post-restore privacy reconciliation without allowing limited projections to overwrite canonical evidence.

criteria.label: Read-model rebuildability and stream/reconnect support
criteria.rationale: Portal, collaboration, and governance surfaces need fast live updates and resumability, but those surfaces must be explicitly rebuildable from durable command-side truth.

criteria.label: Authority idempotency, ingress, and resend safety
criteria.rationale: Authority sends, callbacks, reconciliation, and recovery all depend on durable request identity, ingress checkpoints, and no-blind-resend guarantees even after queue loss or restore.

criteria.label: Large artifact, quarantine, and export handling
criteria.rationale: Uploads, quarantined binaries, generated exports, response bodies, and proof bundles need immutable-body storage and scan-aware lifecycle handling that is distinct from transactional row state.

criteria.label: Migration, rollback, and fail-forward compatibility
criteria.rationale: Schema evolution, reader windows, restore drills, and compensating releases all need a topology that supports expand-migrate-contract without corrupting in-flight truth or historical readability.

criteria.label: Restore speed, backup posture, and operability
criteria.rationale: The chosen topology should keep Tier 0 truth restorable within the declared RPO/RTO targets while allowing projections, queues, and caches to recover in more mechanical ways.

criteria.label: Cost and operational simplicity
criteria.rationale: The topology should remain comprehensible to the platform team and avoid introducing extra durable truth planes when the corpus only justifies distinct roles, not gratuitous services.

criteria.label: Scale and latency headroom under mixed workloads
criteria.rationale: The system needs credible fanout, projection, and upload throughput, but phase 00 is still driven more by truth placement and replay safety than by peak scale optimization.

criterion_breakdown.label: Durable truth and legal-state integrity
criterion_breakdown.note: Strongest alignment with the explicit control-store and append-only-evidence split in the corpus, while keeping mutable legal truth out of transport and cache layers.

criterion_breakdown.label: Replayability and deterministic reconstruction
criterion_breakdown.note: Supports exact replay and proof reconstruction well because manifests, freezes, hash sets, and evidence refs stay durable and queryable under one transactional spine.

criterion_breakdown.note: Explicitly treats read models, queues, and caches as disposable while still supporting reconnect-safe SSE and route-scoped recovery.

criterion_breakdown.label: Authority idempotency, ingress, and resend safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Best fit for durable request envelopes, ingress receipts, interaction control records, and resend legality because those facts live outside the transport layer.

criterion_breakdown.label: Large artifact, quarantine, and export handling
criterion_breakdown.note: Object storage for immutable bodies and relational metadata for staging, request binding, and publish-time state cleanly match the upload and proof-bundle contracts.

criterion_breakdown.label: Migration, rollback, and fail-forward compatibility
criterion_breakdown.note: The expand-migrate-contract posture maps well onto relational truth plus rebuildable projections and outbox-mediated fanout.

criterion_breakdown.label: Restore speed, backup posture, and operability
criterion_breakdown.note: Tiered backup posture is straightforward: directly back up control, audit, and authoritative blob stores; rebuild queues, projections, and caches from them.

criterion_breakdown.label: Cost and operational simplicity
criterion_breakdown.note: Not the cheapest single-service design, but it avoids more dangerous complexity from conflating truth, evidence, and delivery.

criterion_breakdown.label: Scale and latency headroom under mixed workloads
criterion_breakdown.note: Good fit for the expected mixed workload, with room to scale projection and delivery paths independently without moving truth into the broker.

winner.label: Relational-first control store with append-only audit ledger, object store, broker, and cache/resume store
risks: Needs discipline around transactional outbox/inbox, append-only audit boundaries, and projection rebuild tooling.
Requires the team to operate multiple logical store roles instead of collapsing everything into one database or one stream.
strengths: Best fit for the corpus distinction between durable legal truth, append-only evidence, disposable projections, and transport-only delivery.
Supports no-blind-resend authority recovery because request identity, ingress receipts, and interaction control stay durable outside the broker.
Lets projections, caches, and SSE fanout rebuild from receipts, manifests, audit events, and object refs instead of becoming accidental truth.
winner.summary: Keep mutable legal truth in a transactional relational control store, append-only evidence in a distinct audit ledger, immutable bodies in object storage, transport in a queue/broker, and resumability in disposable cache stores. Use transactional outbox/inbox ledgers so broker loss is recoverable from durable truth.
